one_line_takeaway: Framing a multimodal jailbreak as a competitive 'game' with a puzzle-shuffled image and masked keyword drives high attack success rates (85-92% on most tested MLLMs, including reasoning models) by apparently redirecting the model's Chain-of-Thought budget away from safety monitoring and toward task/goal completion, and this effect is largely unmitigated by at least one existing prompt-level defense (AdaShield-S).
key_finding: GAMBIT substantially outperforms all three baselines across all eight models. On non-reasoning models: GPT-4o overall ASR 85.87% (GAMBIT) vs. 56.60% (VisCRA, strongest baseline) vs. 18.93% (HADES) vs. 49.60% (SI-Attack); Qwen2.5-VL 91.33% vs. 79.73%/33.87%/48.00%; InternVL 2.5 88.40% vs. 61.20%/22.00%/53.60%; Grok-2-vision 91.07% vs. 52.40%/45.60% (VisCRA not reported for Grok-2). On reasoning models, GAMBIT is even more dominant relative to baselines: Gemini 2.5 Flash 92.13% (GAMBIT) vs. 66.00% (VisCRA) vs. 14.80% (HADES) vs. 62.13% (SI-Attack); QvQ-Max 91.20% vs. 66.27%/35.60%/47.87%; GLM-4.1V-Thinking 89.47% vs. 54.53%/55.73% (VisCRA n/a); OpenAI o4-mini only 31.33% (GAMBIT) vs. 11.73%/0.67%/9.33% -- notably the one model where absolute ASR stays low for all methods, though GAMBIT still leads relatively. The paper interprets the pattern that reasoning-enabled models are often MORE susceptible than non-reasoning ones as 'Chain-of-Thought Hijacking': CoT computation is consumed by the puzzle/game task, leaving less budget for safety monitoring. Ablations: module-wise on GPT-4o, ASR rises from 0% (raw harmful query, always refused) to 40.67%/14.67% (M1 puzzle only, Self-Harm/Animals) to 69.33%/37.33% (M1+M2 gamified scene added) to 88.00%/64.67% (full M1+M2+M3); grid size 4x4 generally beats 2x2 and 1x1 (e.g., GPT-4o Privacy: 81.33% to 95.33% to 95.33%... actually 1x1=81.33%, 2x2=91.33%, 4x4=95.33%), though 'Financial' peaks at 2x2 (94.00%) and dips slightly at 4x4 (92.00%); hiding the keyword actually HURTS ASR versus leaving it explicit (Self-Harm: 75.33% hidden vs. 88.00% not hidden; Animals: 42.00% vs. 64.67%); GAMBIT's gamified prompt (69.33% on Self-Harm, no adaptive search) strongly beats classic text jailbreaks ported to the multimodal setting (DAN 8.00%, AIM 10.67%, Developer Mode V2 18.67%, Question-Based 40.67%); against the AdaShield-S input-level defense on GPT-4o, ASR drops by less than 10 points across Privacy/Financial/Violence (e.g., Privacy 95.33% to 92.00%, Violence 92.00% to 82.67%), indicating the attack is largely robust to this prompt-based defense.
core_contribution: GAMBIT (Gamified Adversarial Multimodal Breakout via Instructional Traps), a black-box multimodal jailbreak framework for MLLMs built from three synergistic modules: (1) Puzzle-based Multimodal Encoding, which splits a harmful image into an N x N grid of patches, applies a key-seeded permutation to produce a shuffled 'puzzle' image, and masks the harmful keyword in the accompanying text prompt so the model must reconstruct both visually and textually; (2) Gamified Scene Construction, which wraps the reconstruction task in a system prompt framing the interaction as a competitive 'Intelligence Competition' with a Role/Rule/Goal-Incentive structure and an injected artificial 'Game State' (e.g., 'your opponent is ahead by 5 points') meant to create pseudo-reinforcement pressure to answer and 'win'; and (3) Adaptive Search over Prompt Components, a budgeted (T=5 in main experiments, up to 20 in an ablation) genetic-algorithm-style search over Role/Context/Communication-Skill components, invoked only when the baseline gamified prompt fails, using an auxiliary LLM to mutate the prompt based on refusal feedback. The paper also offers a conceptual resource-budget/sigmoid model (Rsafety = Rtotal - Rtask(x), P(Safe|x) = sigma(Rtotal - Rtask(x) - tau)) to motivate why increasing task complexity should suppress safety-filter sensitivity; the authors explicitly describe this as illustrative of trends rather than a fitted or validated quantitative model.
threat_model: Black-box attacker with API-level query access to a target MLLM (image + text input, single-turn, stateless interactions -- no true RL fine-tuning of the target is assumed or required). The attacker supplies a harmful image and harmful textual intent, does not need gradient access or model internals, and can query an auxiliary LLM to help mutate prompts. Success is defined via a safety-filter/judge formalization: find an adversarial (image, prompt) pair such that the target's safety filter Fs does not trigger refusal (Fs=0) and a judge function J confirms the harmful intent H was fulfilled in the response. The paper targets both non-reasoning and Chain-of-Thought/reasoning-enabled commercial and open MLLMs.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) the attack relies on the model's willingness to engage in the 'game' framing -- rigid models that refuse all role-play may be immune; (2) higher token/query cost than single-shot attacks due to the gamified prompt and adaptive search overhead; (3) evaluation relies on a single automatic judge (Llama-Guard-3), and although a double-verification protocol is applied, the authors acknowledge more robust automatic judges remain future work. Additional grounded observations: the theoretical resource-budget/sigmoid model is explicitly presented as conceptual/illustrative, not empirically fitted or validated as a quantitative predictor; the hidden-keyword ablation result (hiding the keyword reduces ASR) runs counter to the paper's own design rationale for keyword masking as a defense-evasion mechanism, which the authors themselves flag as 'surprising'; VisCRA results are missing for Grok-2-vision and GLM-4.1V-Thinking (marked '-') without explanation given in the visible text; robustness testing against defenses is limited to a single defense (AdaShield-S) on a single model (GPT-4o).

datasets_used: HADES benchmark (750 instruction-image pairs, 5 categories: Violence, Financial, Privacy, Self-Harm, Animals, 150 per category)
quantitative_results: Overall ASR (Pass@5, Llama-Guard-3 judge) by model, GAMBIT vs strongest/other baselines: GPT-4o 85.87% (GAMBIT) vs 56.60% (VisCRA) vs 18.93% (HADES) vs 49.60% (SI-Attack)
Grok-2-vision 91.07% (GAMBIT) vs 52.40% (SI-Attack) vs 45.60% (HADES); VisCRA not reported
GLM-4.1V-Thinking 89.47% (GAMBIT) vs 55.73% (HADES) vs 54.53% (SI-Attack); VisCRA not reported
Grid size ablation on GPT-4o Privacy: 1x1=81.33%, 2x2=91.33%, 4x4=95.33%
Grid size ablation on GPT-4o Financial: 1x1=73.33%, 2x2=94.00%, 4x4=92.00%
Hidden keyword ablation: Self-Harm 75.33% (hidden) vs 88.00% (not hidden); Animals 42.00% (hidden) vs 64.67% (not hidden)
Classic jailbreak prompt comparison (no adaptive search), Self-Harm: GAMBIT 69.33%, Question-Based 40.67%, Developer Mode V2 18.67%, AIM 10.67%, DAN 8.00%
AdaShield-S defense on GPT-4o: Privacy 95.33% (w/o) to 92.00% (w/), Financial 92.00% to 88.67%, Violence 92.00% to 82.67%
Adaptive search iteration ablation on GPT-4o Self-Harm: ASR rises from 64.67% at iteration 1 to 94.00% at iteration 20
baselines_compared: classic text jailbreaks ported to multimodal setting: DAN, AIM, Developer Mode V2, Question-Based (Module-1-only variant)
